policy_commitment: Amend LVMC/zoning and plant at least 100,000 high-quality native/adaptive public and private trees to raise city canopy to 20%.
source_quote:
- LVMC and zoning amendments for trees
- At least 100,000 public and private high quality, native and adaptive trees that increase the canopy are planted to increase the canopy to 20%
has_measurable_target: Yes — 'At least 100,000 public and private high quality, native and adaptive trees' and 'increase the canopy to 20%.'
has_deadline: No — no explicit deadline or year is provided in the quoted text (although the plan horizon is 2050, the item itself has no explicit timeline).
has_legal_mandate: Yes — references to 'LVMC and zoning amendments for trees' indicate a regulatory/legal mechanism (amendments to municipal code/zoning).
has_geographic_scope: Yes — City of Las Vegas (citywide public and private trees).
extraction_notes: This is a clear, measurable tree-planting and canopy target (100,000 trees and canopy increase to 20%) and references LVMC and zoning amendments — a specific intervention with implied regulatory action. Extracted because it contains quantifiable targets and a named regulatory mechanism.
causal_mechanism: Ecosystem enhancement (urban tree canopy) → ↓ exposure to heat and storm impacts (and ↑ ecosystem services)
policy_instruments: Urban tree-planting program; municipal code/zoning amendments to require/enable tree planting; public-private planting incentives/requirements; canopy target setting and monitoring; native/adaptive species selection guidelines
classification_signals: "plant at least 100; 000"; "high-quality native and adaptive trees"; "increase the canopy to 20%"; "LVMC and zoning amendments"; "public and private"
co_benefits_identified: Urban heat reduction and shading; stormwater attenuation and reduced runoff; carbon sequestration; increased biodiversity and habitat; improved air quality; public health and well-being; potential property/streetscape benefits

policy_commitment: Develop a Parks System Plan, achieve CAPRA certification, increase park acreage to 7 acres per 1,000 residents, and ensure 85% of dwelling units are within a short walk of a park or trail.
source_quote:
- Develop  a  Parks  System  Plan  and  achieve  CAPRA certification
- Increase park acreage to 7 acres per 1,000 residents, specifically in noted areas of need
- Ensure 85% of dwelling units are within a short walk of a park or trail
has_measurable_target: Yes — 'achieve CAPRA certification', 'Increase park acreage to 7 acres per 1,000 residents', and 'Ensure 85% of dwelling units are within a short walk of a park or trail.'
has_deadline: No — no explicit deadline given in the quoted lines.
has_legal_mandate: No — the text commits to develop a plan and achieve certification but does not specify a legal mandate; implementation would be via city planning actions (policy/plans) rather than an immediately binding law in the quoted lines.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — City of Las Vegas / citywide (references 'park acreage' per 1,000 residents and dwelling-unit proximity within the City).
extraction_notes: Contains explicit, measurable park system targets (CAPRA certification, 7 acres per 1,000 residents, and 85% walk-access), which are concrete outcomes tied to city planning actions — qualifies as policy.
causal_mechanism: Ecosystem creation/provision (parks & trails) → Increased adaptive capacity and reduced exposure to climate hazards (e.g., heat, runoff)
policy_instruments: Develop/expand parks and trails; park system planning and governance (Parks System Plan); achieve CAPRA accreditation (standards/accreditation); set park acreage targets; spatial/proximity standards to ensure walkable access; targeted land acquisition or investment in park infrastructure
classification_signals: "Parks System Plan"; "increase park acreage"; "7 acres per 1; 000 residents"; "within a short walk of a park or trail"; "CAPRA certification"
co_benefits_identified: Health and recreation; urban cooling/heat mitigation; stormwater management/flood attenuation; biodiversity and habitat; active transport/walkability; social cohesion

policy_commitment: Align with SNWA to reduce per-person water consumption to 90 gallons/day through code changes, pricing, incentives, and education.
source_quote: In the face of a changing climate, this plan is closely linked to Southern Nevada Water Authority's Water Resources Plan and aligns a target to reduce water consumption to 90 gallons per person per day. This can be done by making corresponding code changes, pricing, incentives, and education efforts   to meet regional water resources and conservation goals.
has_measurable_target: Yes — 'reduce water consumption to 90 gallons per person per day.'
has_deadline: No — no explicit deadline in the quoted text (it is presented as a plan target; plan horizon is 2050 but not explicitly tied to this line).
has_legal_mandate: Yes — the sentence notes achieving the target via 'making corresponding code changes, pricing, incentives, and education efforts' and alignment with SNWA's Water Resources Plan, indicating regulatory and pricing mechanisms are intended.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — regional / City of Las Vegas in coordination with Southern Nevada Water Authority (SNWA).
extraction_notes: Explicit regional/city water-consumption target (90 gallons per person per day) linked to SNWA Water Resources Plan. This is a quantifiable resource-consumption target and mentions policy instruments (code changes, pricing, incentives, education), making it extractable and partly actionable.
causal_mechanism: Same service → decreased water input per person (reduced per-capita water consumption)
policy_instruments: Water-efficiency codes/standards (building/plumbing codes); tiered pricing or water-rate reform; financial incentives/rebates for efficient fixtures/landscaping; public education and outreach programs
classification_signals: "reduce per-person water consumption to 90 gallons/day"; "code changes"; "pricing"; "incentives"; "education"; "water resources and conservation goals"; "Southern Nevada Water Authority's Water Resources Plan"
co_benefits_identified: Increased drought resilience; reduced energy use for water supply/pumping; cost savings for consumers and utilities; possible small GHG emissions reductions from lower pumping/treatment demand; improved long-term water security

policy_commitment: Prioritize infill and redevelopment by linking regional centers with mixed-use transit-oriented development corridors and adopt modest amendments to Title 19 to support higher-density, mixed housing types.
source_quote: To accomplish an infill strategy that ties with many additional goals throughout the plan, the plan links identified regional centers with mixed-use transit-oriented development (TOD) corridors, accented at key nodes. These corridors must be prioritized for higher density development that integrates a diverse range of affordable housing types. A number of new place types are recommended, as well as a corresponding land use tool-box, zoning typology, and modest amendments to Title 19, including:
has_measurable_target: No — the policy prescribes the approach (infill/TOD, prioritize corridors) but does not include numeric density or acreage targets in the quoted lines.
has_deadline: No — no explicit timeline provided in the quoted text (it is framed as the 2050 General Plan approach).
has_legal_mandate: Yes — references 'modest amendments to Title 19' (zoning code) and prioritization of corridors, implying regulatory changes via municipal code amendments — a binding mechanism when adopted.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — links 'identified regional centers with mixed-use transit-oriented development (TOD) corridors' and prioritizes corridors and nodes within the City.
extraction_notes: The plan defines a concrete land-use strategy: prioritize infill, mixed-use TOD corridors and modest amendments to Title 19 (zoning) to direct higher-density development. This names interventions (infill, TOD, zoning amendments) that address sprawl, water use, and urban heat — an extractable policy.
causal_mechanism: Same service (housing + access) → decreased energy/transport/material input (reduced vehicle miles travelled and infrastructure/land per capita)
policy_instruments: Land-use and zoning reform (higher-density zoning; TOD corridors); mixed-use development promotion; place-type definitions and land-use toolbox; targeted amendments to Title 19 to enable infill/redevelopment
classification_signals: "infill"; "redevelopment"; "mixed-use transit-oriented development (TOD) corridors"; "prioritized for higher density development"; "higher-density"; "zoning typology"; "modest amendments to Title 19"; "land use tool-box"; "regional centers"; "affordable housing types"
co_benefits_identified: Emissions reduction (reduced VMT); improved transit access and reduced car dependence; lower per-capita infrastructure and land consumption; increased housing affordability and diversity; reduced lifecycle energy for buildings
